data.short_reference: It's a tool that automates code releases by organizing steps like testing and deployment into stages, using other AWS services to handle the actual work.

data.definition: Is a managed build service that compiles source code, runs tests, and produces deployable artifacts in ephemeral, fully managed build environments defined by buildspec.yml files. Build environments come in standard (Amazon Linux, Ubuntu, Windows) or custom Docker image variants, with configurable compute sizes. It eliminates Jenkins infrastructure maintenance and integrates natively with CodePipeline, ECR, and S3.
data.code_example:
# buildspec.yml snippet
phases:
  install:
    commands: [pip install -r requirements.txt]
  build:
    commands: [pytest tests/ -v]
artifacts:
  files: ['**/*']
data.short_reference: It automatically builds and tests code using a setup file, then packages the output into deployable files using pre-configured environments. It works with other AWS tools to handle the whole process without needing extra setup.

data.definition: Automates application deployments to EC2 instances, on-premises servers, Lambda functions, and ECS services. For EC2 deployments, the agent on each instance executes lifecycle event hooks defined in appspec.yml (BeforeInstall, AfterInstall, ApplicationStart, ApplicationStop). For Lambda and ECS, it handles canary, linear, and all-at-once traffic shifting strategies with automatic rollback on CloudWatch alarm violations.
data.short_reference: This automates deploying apps to EC2, Lambda, and ECS by managing the deployment process and handling traffic shifts with automatic rollbacks if issues arise.

data.definition: This is a managed Docker and OCI container image registry that stores, manages, and deploys container images in AWS. Images are stored with AES-256 encryption at rest and access is controlled via IAM resource policies. It integrates directly with ECS, EKS, and CodeBuild. Lifecycle policies automatically clean up untagged or old images to control storage costs — a common oversight that leads to substantial bills.
data.code_example: aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com && docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/my-app:latest
data.short_reference: It's a service that stores and manages container images securely in AWS, using encryption and permissions, and works with tools like ECS and EKS to handle deployments and clean up old images to save costs.

data.definition: This is a hierarchical key-value store for configuration data and secrets within AWS Systems Manager. Standard parameters are free; Advanced parameters support larger values and parameter policies (expiration, notifications). SecureString parameters are encrypted with KMS. Compared to Secrets Manager, it is less expensive and lacks automatic rotation — use Secrets Manager for credentials that must rotate, this service for static configuration that needs secure storage.
data.short_reference: It's a storage system in AWS for keeping settings and secrets safe, using encryption for sensitive info. It's cheaper than another service but doesn't automatically update secrets, so it's good for things that don't change much.

data.definition: Allows organizations to create and manage catalogs of approved IT service templates that end users can self-service deploy, without requiring direct AWS console or API access to provision raw resources. Portfolio administrators define products (CloudFormation templates) with constraints (launch roles, tag enforcement, resource limits), and users deploy pre-approved, compliant infrastructure. It enforces governance without blocking developer velocity.
data.short_reference: It's a tool that lets admins set up pre-approved IT services with rules, so users can deploy them without needing direct AWS access, keeping things secure while letting developers work faster.

data.definition: This is a configuration management service offering managed instances of Chef and Puppet. It handles the operational work of running Chef/Puppet masters in AWS. The older layer-based model for defining application stacks uses Chef recipes. It is largely superseded by Systems Manager and CDK for new projects but remains relevant for organizations with existing Chef/Puppet investment.
data.short_reference: It's a service that automates server setups using Chef or Puppet in AWS, handling server management tasks. It's mostly replaced by newer tools but still used by teams already invested in Chef or Puppet.

data.definition: This is a managed Git repository service hosted within AWS, providing private Git repositories with IAM-based authentication and no storage or bandwidth limits. It integrates natively with CodePipeline, CodeBuild, and other developer tools. Note: AWS announced in 2024 that it is no longer accepting new customers, signaling a shift toward GitHub/GitLab integrations — existing customers continue to use it but new architectures should use GitHub Actions or GitLab CI with CodeBuild/CodePipeline integrations.
data.short_reference: It's a managed Git service on AWS for private repositories with IAM login and unlimited storage, working well with tools like CodePipeline and CodeBuild.
